turns.content: I've always wondered what the difference is between implementing the Extend trait and using FromIterator for collecting items into a Vec.

Extend is specifically designed for bulk insertion when you have an iterator that yields elements of the same type, while FromIterator allows any collection to implement IntoIterator so it can be converted to a Vec. The key distinction is that Extend operates on an existing instance and adds to it, whereas FromIterator enables conversion from one container shape into another.
That sounds complicated and I'm still confused about when exactly to use which trait instead of just sticking with standard collections like HashMap or HashSet.

You should implement Extend when you need to add multiple items at once without looping manually, such as adding all elements from a range into a custom list. For FromIterator, use it whenever you want to transform an existing collection into a different type like converting a Vec of strings into a HashSet.
Can I implement both traits at the same time or do they conflict with each other in my custom struct?

They don't inherently conflict, but you only need to implement one depending on your use case. If you want to add bulk items using iterators without manual loops, extend is more convenient than relying on FromIterator alone for that specific operation.
Wait so if I'm building a custom collection and need to insert many items at once, should I always use Extend or can I just rely on standard library methods?

If you're implementing your own collection type, you'll likely want to implement Extend so you can perform bulk insertion efficiently without writing explicit loops. Standard methods like push() are fine for single items but become inefficient when scaling up.

I'm back after five years away from Rust. I'm trying to fix my CI pipeline where the tests fail because my custom Node structure isn't being collected into a vector correctly. The error message says 'failed to collect items' when calling `collect::<Vec<_>>()` on an iterator that should have been created via `Extend`. Can you help me figure out why this is failing?

The issue stems from how your custom Node type implements the Extend trait. When you call `collect::<Vec<_>>()`, it relies on the iterator yielding values that match the expected element type, but if your Node struct is missing required fields or has incorrect lifetime annotations, the compiler cannot guarantee the safety of this operation. The Extend trait specifically requires that every item yielded by the iterator satisfies the constraints of the target collection, and without proper implementation details, Rust's borrow checker rejects the unsafe bulk insertion.

I thought I had implemented Extend correctly on Node. But maybe I confused it with FromIterator? I tried using `FromIterator` to collect items into a Vec<Node>, but that doesn't seem to work either because my iterator isn't producing enough data.

You are conflating two different traits. FromIterator is used when you want to construct a collection directly from an existing iterator, whereas Extend allows you to add items to an already constructed collection without creating a new instance. If your Node struct has a default constructor that isn't public or lacks the necessary fields for cloning, then neither trait can function as intended because the compiler cannot prove that the resulting Vec<Node> is valid.
Okay so maybe I need to make sure Node implements Clone and Debug? But what about the interplay between Iterator and these traits?

The core concept here is that Extend is essentially a specialized form of FromIterator designed for adding items to an existing collection. When you implement Extend, you are effectively telling Rust how to bulk-insert into your custom data structure without needing to manually loop through each element. The Iterator trait provides the mechanism by which individual items are yielded, and when combined with Extend or FromIterator, they allow efficient aggregation of these items into a final container like Vec<Node>.
